qa_pairs.question: When using the `pdb` debugger for Python, what are some of the most useful commands for navigating and inspecting code?
qa_pairs.answer: Some of the most useful `pdb` commands include `w` (to print a stack trace), `b` (to set a breakpoint), `s` (to step into a function), `n` (to continue to the next line in the current function), `r` (to continue until the current function returns), `c` (to continue until a breakpoint is hit), `p exp` (to evaluate an expression), `l` (to list source code for the current file), and `q` (to quit the debugger).

qa_pairs.question: What are the recommended methods for diagnosing a hanging Python script on a cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: To diagnose a hanging Python script, you can either use the `faulthandler` module to dump a traceback after a timeout (by editing your script beforehand) or use `py-spy` to inspect a running process without prior modification. `py-spy` allows you to see live feedback on where your code is spending time (`py-spy top --pid PID`) or get a traceback of its current state (`py-spy dump --pid PID`).

qa_pairs.question: How can a user resolve errors such as 'Package 'X' requires a different Python' or 'Package has requirement X, but you'll have Y which is incompatible' when installing Python packages?
qa_pairs.answer: If a package requires a different Python version, you should update to a more recent Python module or install an older version of the package. For incompatible dependency errors, it is recommended to upgrade `pip` to the latest version (or higher than 21.3) using `pip install --no-index --upgrade pip` and then retry the installation command.
